title: Making Health Insurance More Affordable: Q&A with Christine Eibner
gist: RAND health economist Christine Eibner argues US health coverage is now eroding due to expiring pandemic tax credits and the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, that popular state cost-growth benchmarks demonstrably fail to lower prices, and that the real savings lever is cutting administrative complexity and unbundling routine care from catastrophic insurance.

themes.theme: Coverage erosion from expiring subsidies and OBBBA

insights.claim: Pandemic-era health insurance tax credits expired at the end of 2025, and more than 2.5 million fewer people enrolled in ACA marketplace plans for 2026, with the decline expected to continue as premium bills arrive.
insights.speaker: Christine Eibner
insights.so_what: A reversal of record-low uninsured rates signals rising bad-debt and uncompensated-care exposure for hospitals and lower membership for marketplace insurers.
insights.quote: More than 2.5 million fewer people enrolled in marketplace plans for 2026, and that decline will likely continue as people start to get their bills and have to pay their premiums.
insights.locator: We're starting to see the numbers creep up

insights.claim: Medicaid work requirements and other funding changes in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act are projected to leave about 7.6 million fewer people enrolled in Medicaid by 2034.
insights.speaker: Christine Eibner
insights.so_what: A structural shrinking of the Medicaid pool pressures managed-care organizations' membership and shifts costs onto safety-net and rural providers.
insights.quote: There will be about 7.6 million fewer people enrolled in Medicaid by 2034.
insights.locator: In addition to that, work requirements for Medicaid

insights.claim: The share of Americans without health insurance, which had fallen to record lows, is now beginning to climb again as policy subsidies unwind.
insights.speaker: Christine Eibner
insights.so_what: An inflection in the uninsured trend is a leading indicator for provider revenue mix and self-pay/charity-care burden.
insights.locator: We're starting to see the numbers creep up

insights.claim: State Medicaid programs are estimated to see roughly $665 billion in total federal spending reductions by 2034 under the new law.
insights.speaker: Christine Eibner
insights.so_what: A multi-hundred-billion-dollar federal pullback strains state budgets and hospital finances, a headwind for muni credit and non-profit hospital systems in Medicaid-heavy states.
insights.quote: We estimated that state Medicaid funds will see around $665 billion in total federal spending reductions by 2034.
insights.locator: For states, the impacts are going to be much more complicated

insights.claim: The law provides $50 billion over five years for states to strengthen rural health systems, but for many states this will not compensate for the loss of federal Medicaid funding.
insights.speaker: Christine Eibner
insights.so_what: The rural health fund is too small to offset the funding cliff, keeping rural hospital closure risk elevated.
insights.locator: The law also provides $50 billion distributed over a five-year period

insights.claim: The OBBBA changes will unequivocally reduce federal health spending, but state-level effects are complicated because states avoid funding care for enrollees who fail work requirements.
insights.speaker: Christine Eibner
insights.so_what: Net state impact varies widely, so exposure is state-specific rather than uniform across the sector.
insights.quote: It will unequivocally reduce federal spending on health care.
insights.locator: What is that going to mean at the federal and state level

insights.claim: State cost-growth benchmarks that name-and-shame or penalize payers and providers who exceed spending-growth limits showed no evidence of sustained reductions in insurance premiums or hospital prices.
insights.speaker: Christine Eibner
insights.so_what: A politically popular cost-control tool is empirically ineffective, so states adopting benchmarks pose little pricing threat to hospitals and insurers.
insights.quote: We found no evidence of sustained reductions in any of those outcomes.
insights.locator: I can tell you one that's not very promising

insights.claim: Benchmark caps fail largely because most states lack the enforcement authority to confront payers and providers, and even states with that power rarely use it.
insights.speaker: Christine Eibner
insights.so_what: Enforcement weakness means regulatory-benchmark headlines rarely translate into real pricing pressure.
insights.quote: Most states just have limited ability to enforce their benchmarks.
insights.locator: We analyzed the impact those benchmarks have had

themes.theme: Administrative complexity as the real cost lever

insights.claim: US health-insurance administrative complexity, cost-sharing arrangements, network rules, billing, and eligibility determinations is a key cost driver that could be reduced to save money without harming quality.
insights.speaker: Christine Eibner
insights.so_what: Administrative-simplification is the identified savings pool, positive for claims-automation and payer back-office tech but a revenue risk for billing-complexity intermediaries.
insights.locator: The American health insurance system is really confusing

insights.claim: Reducing administrative complexity carries a core trade-off of fewer consumer choices, because the complexity exists to support the range of plan choices.
insights.speaker: Christine Eibner
insights.so_what: Any simplification reform will be politically constrained by choice-reduction backlash, limiting how fast savings can be realized.
insights.quote: The reason we have so much complexity is that we have a lot of different choices.
insights.locator: But a key trade-off there would be choice

insights.claim: Americans expect too much from health insurance, treating it as a payer for minor, predictable care like routine checkups rather than as protection against catastrophic financial risk, which increases overall costs.
insights.speaker: Christine Eibner
insights.so_what: A shift toward catastrophic-only coverage models would reshape demand for supplemental primary-care and direct-pay offerings.
insights.quote: Traditionally, insurance was meant to protect people from catastrophic financial risk.
insights.locator: Do Americans expect too much from health insurance

insights.claim: Covering primary care separately from catastrophic insurance could free patients from restrictive provider networks and increase choice, since networks exist partly as an insurer cost-containment strategy.
insights.speaker: Christine Eibner
insights.so_what: Unbundling primary care is a structural tailwind for direct primary care and cash-pay clinic models.
insights.locator: Maybe if primary care was just covered separately

insights.claim: Unbundling routine and preventative care from insurance risks people foregoing that care, which would worsen long-run health outcomes, a trade-off inherent to every health-reform idea.
insights.speaker: Christine Eibner
insights.so_what: Foregone-care risk means unbundling reforms may raise downstream acute-care costs, complicating the savings case.
insights.quote: If you don't have the preventative and routine care bundled in, people might forego that care.
